behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: Which structural component of a virion is made mainly of protein and is antigenic?
questions.explanation: The capsid is the protein shell (capsomers) — protects the genome, gives shape, binds receptors and is antigenic. Recurrent exam answer.

questions.stem: A virus with a segmented (−)ssRNA genome capable of reassortment / antigenic shift is:
questions.explanation: Influenza (Orthomyxo) has a segmented (−)ssRNA genome → reassortment → antigenic shift → pandemics. Rotavirus is also segmented but dsRNA.

questions.stem: Which statement about (+)ssRNA viruses is correct?

options.A: They must carry their own polymerase to be infectious
options.B: Their genome acts directly as mRNA and is infectious alone
options.C: They always integrate into host DNA

questions.explanation: (+)ssRNA acts directly as mRNA, so naked genome is infectious. (−)ssRNA must bring its own RNA-dependent RNA polymerase and is not infectious alone.

questions.stem: Reverse transcriptase activity is found in which pair?
questions.explanation: Retroviruses (HIV, HTLV) and HBV (Hepadna, via an RNA intermediate) both use reverse transcriptase — a classic exam trap since HBV is a DNA virus.

questions.stem: Which virus CANNOT be propagated in conventional in-vitro cell culture?
questions.explanation: HPV cannot be cultured in vitro; diagnosis is molecular. Viruses in general grow only on living substrates (cell cultures, eggs, animals), never cell-free agar.

questions.stem: Viral tropism is determined primarily by:

options.B: Match between viral attachment protein and host cell receptor

questions.explanation: Tropism = viral attachment protein ↔ specific host receptor, restricting a virus to specific tissues of specific species.

questions.stem: SARS-CoV-2 enters host cells using which receptor?
questions.explanation: SARS-CoV-2 Spike binds ACE2. Influenza HA ↔ sialic acid; HIV gp120 ↔ CD4; EBV/CMV ↔ CD21.

questions.stem: The 'eclipse phase' of viral replication refers to the period when:

options.B: No infectious virus is detectable inside the cell
options.D: The virus is latent for years

questions.explanation: After uncoating, no complete infectious virions can be recovered from the cell until assembly — the eclipse phase.

questions.stem: Naked (non-enveloped) viruses are typically released by:
questions.explanation: Naked viruses are released by lysis (cytolytic). Enveloped viruses bud and may shed without killing the cell → persistent infection.

questions.stem: Antigenic drift differs from antigenic shift in that drift is caused by:
questions.explanation: Drift = small point mutations → seasonal epidemics. Shift = reassortment/major change → pandemics.

questions.stem: Which herpesvirus establishes latency in sensory (dorsal root / trigeminal) ganglia?
questions.explanation: HSV-1/2 and VZV become latent in sensory ganglia. EBV latency is in B cells; HHV-8 → Kaposi sarcoma.

questions.stem: Reactivation of latent VZV produces:
questions.explanation: Primary VZV = chickenpox; reactivation from dorsal root ganglia = zoster along a dermatome.

questions.stem: EBV primarily infects which cells and is associated with which malignancy?

options.B: B cells; Burkitt lymphoma / nasopharyngeal carcinoma

questions.explanation: EBV infects B lymphocytes (via CD21) and is linked to Burkitt lymphoma, nasopharyngeal carcinoma and post-transplant lymphoma.

questions.stem: The most common viral cause of congenital infection and sensorineural hearing loss is:
questions.explanation: Congenital CMV is the leading infectious cause of congenital hearing loss and neurodevelopmental deficit.

questions.stem: HHV-8 is the causative agent of:
questions.explanation: HHV-8 (KSHV) causes Kaposi sarcoma, especially in AIDS. HHV-6/7 cause roseola.

questions.stem: Which hepatitis viruses are transmitted mainly by the faecal–oral route?
questions.explanation: HAV and HEV are enteric (faecal–oral). HBV, HCV, HDV are parenteral/blood-borne.
questions.topic: Hepatitis viruses (A, B, C, D, E) + HBV serology

questions.stem: A patient is HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive, anti-HBc negative. This indicates:
questions.explanation: Isolated anti-HBs (with negative anti-HBc) = vaccine-induced immunity. Natural infection would also make anti-HBc positive.
questions.topic: Hepatitis viruses (A, B, C, D, E) + HBV serology

questions.stem: The marker indicating high infectivity / active HBV replication is:
questions.explanation: HBeAg reflects active replication and high infectivity. Its persistence beyond ~3 months suggests progression to chronicity.
questions.topic: Hepatitis viruses (A, B, C, D, E) + HBV serology

questions.stem: Hepatitis D virus is a defective virus that requires which co-infection to replicate?
questions.explanation: HDV is a defective (satellite) virus needing HBsAg from HBV. Only HBV carriers can be infected by HDV.
questions.topic: Hepatitis viruses (A, B, C, D, E) + HBV serology

questions.stem: Which hepatitis virus most frequently progresses to chronic infection?
questions.explanation: HCV becomes chronic in ~75–85% of cases, a leading cause of cirrhosis/HCC. HAV and HEV do not cause chronic infection.
questions.topic: Hepatitis viruses (A, B, C, D, E) + HBV serology

questions.stem: The leading viral cause of bronchiolitis in infants is:
questions.explanation: RSV is the top cause of bronchiolitis and pneumonia in infants; palivizumab is used for prophylaxis in high-risk babies.
questions.topic: Respiratory viruses (Influenza, Paraflu, RSV, Corona, Rhino, Adeno)

questions.stem: Influenza neuraminidase functions to:

options.B: Release progeny virions from the cell surface

questions.explanation: Haemagglutinin binds sialic acid (entry); neuraminidase cleaves sialic acid to release budding virions — the target of oseltamivir/zanamivir.
questions.topic: Respiratory viruses (Influenza, Paraflu, RSV, Corona, Rhino, Adeno)

questions.stem: The largest number of serotypes, causing the common cold, belongs to:
questions.explanation: Rhinoviruses (~100–160 serotypes) are the commonest cause of the common cold, explaining repeated infections.
questions.topic: Respiratory viruses (Influenza, Paraflu, RSV, Corona, Rhino, Adeno)

questions.stem: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) with a barking cough is most classically caused by:
questions.explanation: Parainfluenza viruses are the classic cause of croup with subglottic narrowing and barking cough.
questions.topic: Respiratory viruses (Influenza, Paraflu, RSV, Corona, Rhino, Adeno)

questions.stem: Adenovirus is distinctive among the common respiratory viruses because it:

options.B: Is a naked dsDNA virus that also causes conjunctivitis and gastroenteritis

questions.explanation: Adenovirus is a naked dsDNA virus causing pharyngoconjunctival fever, keratoconjunctivitis and gastroenteritis, unlike the enveloped RNA respiratory viruses.
questions.topic: Respiratory viruses (Influenza, Paraflu, RSV, Corona, Rhino, Adeno)

questions.stem: The most common cause of severe dehydrating gastroenteritis in young children worldwide is:
questions.explanation: Rotavirus (segmented dsRNA, naked) is the leading cause of severe childhood diarrhoea; live oral vaccines exist.
questions.topic: Viral gastroenteritis (Rota, Noro, Adeno, Astro, Sapo)

questions.stem: The commonest cause of epidemic gastroenteritis outbreaks (cruise ships, institutions) in all ages is:
questions.explanation: Norovirus (Calicivirus, (+)ssRNA) causes explosive outbreaks; very low infectious dose and environmental stability.
questions.topic: Viral gastroenteritis (Rota, Noro, Adeno, Astro, Sapo)

questions.stem: Rotavirus causes diarrhoea mainly by:

options.B: Damaging villous enterocytes + NSP4 enterotoxin → malabsorption/secretion

questions.explanation: Rotavirus destroys mature villous tip cells and NSP4 acts as a viral enterotoxin, causing osmotic + secretory diarrhoea.
questions.topic: Viral gastroenteritis (Rota, Noro, Adeno, Astro, Sapo)

questions.stem: Which gastroenteritis viruses are non-enveloped and therefore alcohol-resistant?

options.B: Rota, Noro, Astro and enteric Adeno

questions.explanation: The enteric viruses are all naked (non-enveloped), making them resistant to alcohol hand gels — soap/water and bleach are preferred.
questions.topic: Viral gastroenteritis (Rota, Noro, Adeno, Astro, Sapo)

questions.stem: Enteric adenoviruses associated with childhood diarrhoea are serotypes:
questions.explanation: Adenovirus 40/41 (subgroup F) are the enteric serotypes causing paediatric gastroenteritis.
questions.topic: Viral gastroenteritis (Rota, Noro, Adeno, Astro, Sapo)

questions.stem: Koplik spots are pathognomonic of:
questions.explanation: Koplik spots (buccal mucosa) precede the measles rash and are pathognomonic.

questions.stem: The most feared late complication of measles, appearing years later, is:
questions.explanation: SSPE is a rare, fatal degenerative CNS disease years after measles due to persistent defective virus.

questions.stem: Rubella infection in the first trimester classically causes:
questions.explanation: Congenital rubella syndrome: sensorineural deafness, cataracts, cardiac defects (PDA), and 'blueberry muffin' rash.

questions.stem: A common complication of mumps in post-pubertal males is:
questions.explanation: Mumps orchitis is unilateral usually; bilateral cases can rarely cause sterility. Mumps also causes aseptic meningitis and parotitis.

questions.stem: Measles, mumps and rubella vaccine is what type of vaccine?
questions.explanation: MMR is a live attenuated vaccine → contraindicated in pregnancy and severe immunosuppression.

questions.stem: Which HPV types are most strongly associated with cervical carcinoma?
questions.explanation: HPV 16 and 18 are the principal high-risk oncogenic types; 6 and 11 cause benign genital warts.

questions.stem: The HPV oncoproteins driving malignant transformation act by:

options.A: E6 inactivating p53 and E7 inactivating Rb

questions.explanation: E6 degrades p53 and E7 inactivates Rb, releasing the cell cycle brake → transformation.

questions.stem: Which virus–cancer pairing is INCORRECT?
questions.explanation: Rotavirus is not oncogenic. The others are established virus–cancer associations.

questions.stem: HTLV-1 is associated with:

options.B: Adult T-cell leukaemia/lymphoma & tropical spastic paraparesis

questions.explanation: HTLV-1 (a retrovirus) causes ATLL and HAM/TSP, endemic in Japan and the Caribbean.

questions.stem: The HPV vaccine is based on:
questions.explanation: HPV vaccines use recombinant L1 virus-like particles (VLPs) — no viral DNA, cannot cause infection.

questions.stem: The commonest cause of viral (aseptic) meningitis is:
questions.explanation: Enteroviruses are the leading cause of aseptic meningitis, typically self-limiting.

questions.stem: HSV encephalitis characteristically affects which brain region?
questions.explanation: HSV-1 encephalitis has a predilection for the temporal lobes; it is a treatable emergency with IV aciclovir.

questions.stem: Negri bodies in neurons are diagnostic of:
questions.explanation: Rabies (Rhabdovirus, bullet-shaped) produces cytoplasmic Negri bodies; post-exposure prophylaxis is vaccine + immunoglobulin.

questions.stem: Poliovirus causes paralysis by destroying:
questions.explanation: Poliovirus targets anterior horn motor neurons → asymmetric flaccid paralysis.

questions.stem: Rabies post-exposure prophylaxis in a previously unvaccinated person includes:

options.B: Wound care + rabies vaccine + rabies immunoglobulin

questions.explanation: Once symptomatic, rabies is almost always fatal, so prompt wound cleaning, vaccine and RIG are essential during the incubation period.

questions.stem: Which mosquito transmits dengue, Zika, yellow fever and chikungunya?
questions.explanation: Aedes aegypti is the principal urban vector for these arboviruses (Anopheles → malaria; Culex → West Nile/JE).

questions.stem: A severe complication of secondary dengue infection with a different serotype is:
questions.explanation: Antibody-dependent enhancement on secondary infection with a heterologous serotype predisposes to DHF/DSS.

questions.stem: Zika virus infection during pregnancy is associated with:
questions.explanation: Congenital Zika causes microcephaly and other CNS malformations.

questions.stem: Ebola and Marburg belong to which family?
questions.explanation: Ebola/Marburg are filoviruses (filamentous (−)ssRNA) causing viral haemorrhagic fever with high mortality.

questions.stem: Lassa fever, a rodent-borne haemorrhagic fever, is caused by a virus of which family?
questions.explanation: Lassa virus is an Arenavirus transmitted by Mastomys rodent excreta; ribavirin has some efficacy.

questions.stem: Parvovirus B19 is unique among human DNA viruses because it is:
questions.explanation: B19 is the only ssDNA virus infecting humans — very small and non-enveloped.

questions.stem: Parvovirus B19 has tropism for which cells, explaining its main complication?
questions.explanation: B19 infects erythroid precursors through the P antigen (globoside) → transient aplastic crisis, especially in haemolytic anaemias.

questions.stem: The childhood rash caused by parvovirus B19 is:
questions.explanation: Fifth disease = erythema infectiosum with slapped-cheek facial rash and lacy body rash.

questions.stem: B19 infection in pregnancy can cause:
questions.explanation: Fetal infection → severe anaemia and hydrops fetalis due to red-cell aplasia.

questions.stem: In a patient with hereditary spherocytosis, parvovirus B19 characteristically precipitates:
questions.explanation: By halting erythropoiesis in patients already dependent on high red-cell turnover, B19 causes an aplastic crisis.

questions.stem: HIV primarily infects cells bearing which receptor/co-receptor?
questions.explanation: HIV gp120 binds CD4 plus a chemokine co-receptor (CCR5 or CXCR4). CCR5-Δ32 confers relative resistance.

questions.stem: The recommended initial screening test for HIV is:
questions.explanation: 4th-gen combined p24 antigen/antibody assays shorten the window period; reactive results are confirmed by differentiation assay/Western blot or NAT.

questions.stem: The 'window period' in HIV testing is:

options.A: Time from infection until antibodies/antigen are detectable

questions.explanation: The window period is the early interval after infection before the test marker becomes detectable — a false-negative risk.

questions.stem: Which laboratory value best defines progression to AIDS?
questions.explanation: AIDS is defined by CD4 < 200/µL or an AIDS-defining opportunistic illness.

questions.stem: To monitor the response to antiretroviral therapy, the key test is:
questions.explanation: Plasma HIV-1 RNA (viral load) monitors treatment efficacy; CD4 count tracks immune recovery. Antibody tests are for diagnosis, not monitoring.

questions.stem: Aciclovir is selectively activated in HSV-infected cells by:
questions.explanation: Aciclovir requires viral thymidine kinase for the first phosphorylation, giving selectivity; it is a guanosine analogue chain terminator.

questions.stem: Oseltamivir treats influenza by inhibiting:
questions.explanation: Oseltamivir/zanamivir are neuraminidase inhibitors, blocking release of progeny virions.

questions.stem: Which drug class targets HIV by preventing integration of viral DNA into host DNA?
questions.explanation: Integrase inhibitors (…-tegravir) block proviral integration. NRTIs/NNRTIs target reverse transcriptase; protease inhibitors block maturation.

questions.stem: A first-line direct-acting antiviral cure strategy exists for which chronic hepatitis?
questions.explanation: HCV is now curable with direct-acting antivirals (e.g., sofosbuvir-based). HBV is suppressed but rarely cured (nucleos(t)ide analogues).

questions.stem: Why do antiviral drugs generally have a narrow therapeutic window?

options.B: Viruses use host-cell machinery, so few unique viral targets exist

questions.explanation: Because viruses replicate using host machinery, selective toxicity is hard — drugs target the few virus-specific enzymes.

questions.stem: Which is an example of a live attenuated viral vaccine?
questions.explanation: MMR, oral polio (Sabin), varicella, yellow fever and rotavirus are live attenuated. HBV/HPV are recombinant subunit; Salk polio is inactivated.

questions.stem: The hepatitis B vaccine is produced as:
questions.explanation: HBV vaccine is recombinant HBsAg expressed in yeast — induces protective anti-HBs.

questions.stem: A key contraindication to live attenuated vaccines is:
questions.explanation: Live vaccines can replicate and are generally contraindicated in pregnancy and significant immunodeficiency.

questions.stem: Compared with inactivated vaccines, live attenuated vaccines generally:

options.B: Induce stronger, longer-lasting immunity including cellular immunity

questions.explanation: Live vaccines elicit broad, durable humoral + cellular (and often mucosal) immunity, usually with fewer doses, but carry a small reversion risk.

questions.stem: Passive immunisation (e.g., hepatitis B immunoglobulin after exposure) provides:
questions.explanation: Passive immunity gives immediate, short-lived protection via pre-formed antibodies, with no immunological memory.

questions.stem: The first line of antiviral defence, produced early by infected cells, is:
questions.explanation: Type I interferons induce an antiviral state in neighbouring cells and activate NK cells early in infection.

questions.stem: Which cells kill virus-infected cells that have DOWN-regulated MHC class I?
questions.explanation: NK cells detect 'missing self' (loss of MHC I) — a strategy viruses use to evade CD8 T cells.

questions.stem: Cytotoxic (CD8) T lymphocytes recognise viral peptides presented on:
questions.explanation: Endogenous viral peptides are presented on MHC class I to CD8 cytotoxic T cells, which kill infected cells.

questions.stem: Which antibody class is most important at mucosal surfaces (gut, respiratory tract)?
questions.explanation: Secretory IgA neutralises viruses at mucosal portals of entry — relevant to oral/intranasal vaccines.

questions.stem: Neutralising antibodies protect chiefly by:
questions.explanation: Neutralising antibodies bind surface proteins and prevent attachment/entry; they are the correlate of protection for many vaccines.

questions.stem: In a severely immunocompromised (e.g., transplant) patient, which latent virus commonly reactivates to cause pneumonitis/colitis?
questions.explanation: CMV reactivation is a major cause of pneumonitis, colitis and retinitis in transplant and AIDS patients.

questions.stem: CMV retinitis is most characteristic of AIDS patients when the CD4 count falls below:
questions.explanation: CMV retinitis typically occurs at CD4 < 50/µL, a hallmark of advanced AIDS.

questions.stem: Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) in immunosuppressed patients is caused by:
questions.explanation: Reactivated JC virus infects oligodendrocytes → demyelinating PML, seen in AIDS and with certain immunotherapies.

questions.stem: Which fungal infection is an AIDS-defining opportunistic pneumonia?
questions.explanation: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) is a classic AIDS-defining illness at low CD4 counts.

questions.stem: EBV in profoundly immunosuppressed transplant patients can drive:
questions.explanation: Uncontrolled EBV-driven B-cell proliferation causes PTLD after transplantation.

questions.stem: Which organism is a dimorphic fungus (mould in environment, yeast at body temperature)?
questions.explanation: Dimorphic fungi (Histoplasma, Blastomyces, Coccidioides, Sporothrix) grow as moulds at 25°C and yeasts at 37°C.
questions.topic: Mycology — yeasts, moulds, dimorphic, dermatophytes + antifungals

questions.stem: Aspergillus most characteristically causes disease by:

options.B: Septate hyphae with acute-angle branching invading lung tissue

questions.explanation: Aspergillus shows septate hyphae branching at ~45°, causing aspergilloma and invasive aspergillosis, especially in neutropenia.
questions.topic: Mycology — yeasts, moulds, dimorphic, dermatophytes + antifungals

questions.stem: Cryptococcus neoformans is best identified in CSF by:

options.B: India ink / capsular antigen (halo of the polysaccharide capsule)

questions.explanation: Cryptococcus has a thick polysaccharide capsule seen with India ink; cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) testing is more sensitive. It causes meningitis in AIDS.
questions.topic: Mycology — yeasts, moulds, dimorphic, dermatophytes + antifungals

questions.stem: Dermatophytes (Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton) infect:
questions.explanation: Dermatophytes digest keratin, causing tinea (ringworm) of skin, hair and nails — a superficial mycosis.
questions.topic: Mycology — yeasts, moulds, dimorphic, dermatophytes + antifungals

questions.stem: The 'germ tube' test at 37°C is used to presumptively identify:
questions.explanation: Candida albicans forms germ tubes within a couple of hours in serum at 37°C — a rapid presumptive ID.
questions.topic: Mycology — yeasts, moulds, dimorphic, dermatophytes + antifungals

questions.stem: Malaria is transmitted by which vector, and the most dangerous species is:
questions.explanation: Female Anopheles transmits Plasmodium; P. falciparum causes the most severe (cerebral) malaria.

questions.stem: Toxoplasma gondii's definitive host, and the main risk in pregnancy, is:
questions.explanation: Cats are the definitive host; primary infection in pregnancy risks congenital toxoplasmosis with the classic triad.

questions.stem: A motile, flagellated protozoan causing vaginitis with a frothy discharge is:
questions.explanation: Trichomonas vaginalis is a sexually transmitted flagellate causing frothy, malodorous discharge and 'strawberry cervix'; treated with metronidazole.

questions.stem: Giardia lamblia and Entamoeba histolytica are typically acquired by:
questions.explanation: Both are transmitted by ingesting cysts. Giardia → malabsorptive diarrhoea; E. histolytica → bloody dysentery and liver abscess.

questions.stem: The pinworm causing perianal itching, diagnosed by the adhesive-tape test, is:
questions.explanation: Enterobius (pinworm) causes nocturnal perianal pruritus; eggs are collected on cellophane tape in the morning.

questions.stem: The most sensitive and now first-line method for diagnosing most viral infections is:
questions.explanation: Molecular NAAT/PCR is fast and highly sensitive, replacing culture as the first-line viral diagnostic for most agents.

questions.stem: Detection of virus-specific IgM in serum generally indicates:
questions.explanation: IgM appears early and is a marker of recent/acute infection; IgG indicates past exposure or immunity.

questions.stem: A four-fold rise in IgG titre between acute and convalescent sera indicates:
questions.explanation: A ≥4-fold rise in paired sera confirms recent active infection.

questions.stem: Cytopathic effect (CPE) observed in cell culture is used to:

options.B: Indicate viral growth/replication in the culture

questions.explanation: CPE (rounding, syncytia, inclusion bodies) signals viral replication in cell culture.

questions.stem: Which specimen/method is appropriate for diagnosing a suspected dermatophyte (tinea) infection?

options.B: Skin/nail scraping with KOH prep (and fungal culture)

questions.explanation: Skin/nail scraping with KOH prep reveals hyphae; Sabouraud culture confirms the dermatophyte.
